title: Kerberoasting a Service Account
briefing: The domain controller $TARGET logged a run of Kerberos service-ticket requests (Event ID 4769) for service account(s), all using the weak RC4 encryption type (0x17), from a single internal host. That is the signature of Kerberoasting: an authenticated attacker pulling crackable service tickets offline. Identify the requesting host and the targeted service account(s).
dashboard_hint: Discover -> rule.id: 100120. Filter data.win.eventdata.ticketEncryptionType: 0x17 (RC4). Read serviceName + ipAddress.

questions.explain: 4769 service-ticket requests with RC4 (0x17) encryption for SPN accounts = Kerberoasting for offline cracking.
Every 4769 carries ipAddress = $SRC1 — the compromised internal host to triage.
serviceName shows $SVC1 and $SVC2 — the SPN accounts whose tickets can be cracked.

questions.prompt: Which field most strongly flags this as malicious rather than normal Kerberos traffic?
questions.explain: Modern tickets use AES; a burst of RC4 (0x17) requests is the roasting tell.
T1558.003 Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting.

options: Rotate the affected service account passwords (long, random)
Set service accounts to AES-only / disable RC4
Triage the requesting host for compromise
answer: Rotate the affected service account passwords (long, random)
Set service accounts to AES-only / disable RC4
Triage the requesting host for compromise
questions.explain: Rotate to long random secrets, kill RC4, and investigate the requesting host that is already inside.
